caveats: a: BLS occupation counts cover NAICS 325 rather than this residual six-digit code or the eligible service subset.
a: ILO exposure measures technical potential, not observed substitution, and emphasizes generative AI.
a: Product and process differences within 325199 can materially change the wage-weighted task mix.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation for lower-middle-market 325199 processors.
rho: Customer qualification and data readiness may vary more by product line than by firm size.
e: The provided firm count is a margin-bridged estimate and may include unrelated product economics inside this residual code.
e: Public marketing language such as custom does not establish that outsourced processing is recurring or material revenue.
s5: Census owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
s5: Age and stated succession interest do not establish a transferable operation or completed control sale.

q: Retention can differ sharply between unique customer-owned chemistry and benchmarked bulk products.

d5: A single demand ratio masks divergent end markets within this residual code.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: All Other Basic Organic Chemical Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial customers buy recurring batches, campaigns, or contracted volumes under defined specifications, with revenue based on conversion fees, per-unit pricing, or formulas that may adjust for feedstock and energy.
lens.deviation_from_default: The residual code spans materially different products, including fatty acids, enzyme proteins, organo-inorganic compounds, plasticizers, and silicones, and mixes bulk commodity with custom manufacturing. The lens retains only recurring outsourced toll or custom processing to make the screened business model coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Integrated or captive plants, businesses selling only proprietary commodity output, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market toll, custom, and specification-driven producers that repeatedly synthesize, ferment, purify, recover, or modify basic organic chemicals for external industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for all chemical manufacturing rather than NAICS 325199 custom processors, and ILO reports GenAI task exposure for broad global occupations. I assign higher exposure to sales support, quoting, scheduling, purchasing, laboratory documentation, quality review, regulatory work, and engineering analysis, while assigning lower exposure to equipment operation, maintenance, material movement, sampling, and physical laboratory work. The broad industry mix may understate knowledge work in custom batch plants but overstate it in continuous bulk-organic operations.
caveats: BLS occupation counts cover NAICS 325 rather than this residual six-digit code or the eligible service subset.
ILO exposure measures technical potential, not observed substitution, and emphasizes generative AI.
Product and process differences within 325199 can materially change the wage-weighted task mix.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory across at least ten eligible 325199 toll and custom plants, tagged for current automation and actual time by workflow, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Custom organic production has document- and analysis-heavy workflows around specifications, batch records, certificates, deviations, scheduling, and customer coordination. Physical reaction, separation, maintenance, sampling, and accountable release remain less substitutable, and existing process-control automation is outside the opportunity.

d5.bridge: EIA projects growth in broad bulk-chemical feedstock use and explicitly allocates part of NAICS 325199 natural-gas feedstock to methanol and hydrogen in its industrial model, but it does not measure five-year constant-price demand for eligible outsourced processing across the code's many products. I translate the broad physical-input signal into modest base growth and widen it for industrial cycles, imports, substitution, environmental restrictions, and product-specific end markets. Energy-feedstock growth may overstate service demand if new volume goes to integrated plants, while it may understate bio-based or specialty products not modeled closely.
caveats: EIA's bulk-chemicals model is broader than the eligible 325199 service population and emphasizes energy-intensive products.
A single demand ratio masks divergent end markets within this residual code.
Constant-quality outsourced-service quantity is not directly observed.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level shipment volumes, import penetration, customer forecasts, contract backlog, capacity announcements, and recurring-volume histories for a representative sample of eligible firms would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Basic organic chemicals remain physical inputs to plastics, coatings, detergents, agriculture, electronics, construction, and other industrial products. The residual code is exposed to GDP and manufacturing demand but also to imports, capacity cycles, reformulation, and regulation, so broad sector growth does not ensure every service basket grows.

caveats: The provided firm count is a margin-bridged estimate and may include unrelated product economics inside this residual code.
Public marketing language such as custom does not establish that outsourced processing is recurring or material revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the 198 dataset firms using tolling agreements, customer ownership of feedstock or intellectual property, recurring conversion-fee revenue, product catalogs, captive status, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census definition covers a broad residual set including fatty acids, enzyme proteins, organo-inorganic compounds, plasticizers, and silicone. Many firms manufacture their own products, but the variety and prevalence of batch and specification-driven chemistry make toll or custom production more plausible than in a narrowly commoditized code.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325199 ($4,804,700,193) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325199 ($106,967,694,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $1,460,868 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325199); class <5: 110 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,749,355 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $495,159 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,191,857 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $754,953 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 66 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,290,864 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,673,285 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 132 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,458,106 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,447,705 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $145,673,159 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,235,736 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 114 firms, avg revenue/firm $762,441,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $137,315,684 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 198

caveats: The estimate does not protect specific products from regulation or substitution, which is reflected in d5.
Physical operator requirement can persist while eligible demand shifts to a larger supplier.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy studies, announced captive capacity, supplier-consolidation records, product-substitution programs, and validated autonomous-plant evidence that removes external operational accountability would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Demand that remains requires physical reaction, fermentation, separation, purification, testing, safe handling, wastewater control, and accountable product release. Software can reduce information work but cannot provide the chemical; displacement is primarily customer insourcing, consolidation into integrated plants, or chemical substitution.

caveats: No public contract sample measures productivity pass-through in the eligible population.
Retention can differ sharply between unique customer-owned chemistry and benchmarked bulk products.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract set showing term, renewal cadence, open-book provisions, raw-material and energy indices, yield guarantees, gainsharing, minimum volumes, and historical repricing after productivity improvements would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Customer qualification, proprietary process knowledge, difficult chemistry, and specification consistency can preserve benefits, especially when AI improves yield, quality, or turnaround. Open-book tolling, feedstock formulas, procurement benchmarking, renewal bids, and concentrated customers can transmit visible labor savings.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation for lower-middle-market 325199 processors.
Customer qualification and data readiness may vary more by product line than by firm size.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots in eligible plants covering quoting, campaign scheduling, batch-record review, deviations, maintenance triage, and regulatory reporting, with accepted-output rates, exceptions, labor hours, and validation cost, would materially change implementation.
rho.rationale: Planning, commercial, quality, regulatory, and knowledge workflows can be implemented within five years, but hazardous-process validation, customer change control, auditability, cybersecurity, data fragmentation, and rare failure events constrain deployment. EPA's air and wastewater regimes make traceability and accountable controls operational requirements rather than optional overhead.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older, not completed control transfers in eligible 325199 firms. I use owner age only as succession pressure and reduce it for the five-year window, institutional and multi-owner firms, internal succession, non-sale exits, environmental liabilities, buyer financing, and customer or permit transfer risk. The cross-industry proxy may overstate transfers in capital-intensive plants but understate succession pressure in founder-led specialty producers.
caveats: Census owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Age and stated succession interest do not establish a transferable operation or completed control sale.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year closed-deal records from specialty-chemical databases, state and EPA permit ownership changes, and interviews with chemical M&A advisers and eligible owners would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: The larger estimated firm population creates more potential targets than adjacent niche codes, and founder succession can matter in custom chemistry. A qualifying transfer still requires portable customer contracts, defensible know-how, permits, clean environmental diligence, and limited key-person dependence.

principal_driver: A meaningful custom-processing subset combines recurring customer specifications with document- and analysis-heavy workflows suitable for AI assistance.
principal_weakness: The residual code is highly heterogeneous, so eligibility, demand, commercial capture, and liabilities cannot be inferred reliably without firm-level classification.
